EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into the area during the afternoon and evening hours, interacting with  decent shear and MLCAPE to allow for numerous thunderstorm development. Pockets of damaging winds occurred along with a total of six confirmed tornadoes (three in far southern Lower Michigan and three in northern Indiana).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF-1 tornado was confirmed in Edwardsburg, MI on March 30th, 2025. The tornado started in a field west of Conrad Rd, snapping and uprooting trees as it moved northeast towards the Edwardsburg Primary School, where EF-0 damage was noted. The tornado continued to the northeast and intensified, where several homes and businesses sustained damage on the east side of Pleasant Lake. The Starboard Choice Marina building sustained roof damage, a boat lift and dock were removed from the lake and flipped over, and a few boats were damaged as well, including one that was flipped and lifted over a fence. The most intense damage was seen along Dailey Road, where numerous large trees were snapped and uprooted, with estimated peak wind speeds of 90 mph. Overall, the tornado was on the ground for 3 minutes and had a peak intensity of EF-1. There were also areas of straight-line wind damage noted throughout Edwardsburg, indicative that the tornado was likely embedded within the line of storms that moved through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid weather continued ahead of a cold front arching from Hudson Bay to Iowa. A mesoscale convective vortex developed over Wisconsin early in the morning and crossed Michigan from southwest to northeast, largely dissipating with time. However, remnants of this MCV crossed northeast lower Michigan and resulted in some tree damage near Hubbard Lake around midday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report including images of over two dozen trees uprooted on the east side of Hubbard Lake. Some had fallen on homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and very humid air mass in place created an unstable environment, which, in the presence of the Lake Huron lake breeze and a subtle stationary boundary stretching from the Keweenaw Peninsula to northern Lake Huron, was able to support development of scattered strong to severe storms across parts of northeast Lower Michigan during the late afternoon on the 7th, resulting in primarily large hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of hailstones up to 2 inches in diameter in the Lachine and Flanders areas in west central Alpena county, Michigan. Hail accumulated on decks and resulted in multiple instances of car and home damage, as well as some damage to trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of hailstones up to the size of ping pong balls around the Hubbard Lake area in northern Alcona county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of quarter size hail in the Long Lake Woods area in central Alcona county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The unseasonably hot and humid air mass persisted into the 8th, resulting in development of additional storms driven primarily by daytime heating across northeast Lower Michigan, where low-level convergence was maximized by the lake breeze boundary as well as a very weak surface trough. The most robust storms developed some rotation, indicated by reports of a wall cloud near Rose City, MI, as well as large hail up to the side of golf balls, along with heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of hail up to the size of golf balls in the vicinity of Rose City in northern Ogemaw county, Michigan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms impacted parts of Lenawee and Washtenaw County, with dime size hail also reported in Oakland county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured at Lenawee County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tightly wound low pressure system, complete with strong winds aloft, resulted in development of a second line of storms during the late morning ahead of a strong cold front (70s ahead of the front across NE Lower MI falling into the 20s and 30s with snow across the Keweenaw Peninsula). Storms primarily produced gusty winds up to around 50mph as they crossed the area through midday, but also produced a few brief instances of hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported penny size hail lasting for approximately a minute near Atlanta, MI.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deepening low pressure moving up the Ohio Valley, combined with mid-level forcing and a strong jet aloft, resulted in snowfall accumulations of 5-8 inches, generally south of the M-72 corridor on the 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer near National City reported a storm total snowfall of 7.3 inches by 0900am on the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lake-induced mesolow convergence lake effect snow band crossed the area late in the day on the 13th, resulting in a narrow area of snowfall totals of 5 to 8 inches from Boyne City to Lovells. Highest totals were located at the NWS Gaylord, MI office, where 8.4 inches of snow fell ending 0700am on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highest snowfall totals ended up occurring from Lakes of the North to the Waters area, with 18hr snowfall totals of 8.4 inches at the NWS Gaylord, MI office by 0700am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deepening low pressure moving up the Ohio Valley, combined with mid-level forcing and a strong jet aloft, resulted in snowfall accumulations of 5-8 inches, generally south of the M-72 corridor on the 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Official snow observer for the Houghton Lake area reported a storm total of 7.2inches. Additional reports of 7 inches in the Roscommon and St. Helen areas within the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deepening low pressure moving up the Ohio Valley, combined with mid-level forcing and a strong jet aloft, resulted in snowfall accumulations of 5-8 inches, generally south of the M-72 corridor on the 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported a 24hr snowfall of 8 inches, 6 miles N of Au Gres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent west flow lake effect snow band wiggled back and forth across Whitefish Point late on the 10th into the morning of the 11th, resulting in snowfall totals of 8.4inches at Paradise. This band began to shift westward late by mesoscale forcing due to strengthening temperature gradient between the very cold continental air of Ontario east of Lake Superior, and the lake-modified air over Lake Superior and the eastern UP.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative observer at Paradise reported a snowfall total of 8.4 inches for the 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing surface low crossed the Ohio Valley on the 12th. Moisture wrapped around the north side of this system, combined with east flow lake enhancement, resulted in several inches of snowfall across northeast lower Michigan by the morning of the 13th. Highest totals were focused primarily in Iosco county, where over 7 inches of new snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals in the Sterling area of 7 to as much as 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southwest to northeast oriented boundary crossing the Thumb of Michigan served as focus for development of a couple rounds of long-duration snowfall as waves moved up this boundary with time. Northeast flow resulted in lake enhancement across northeast lower Michigan through the event; winds backing to north and eventually northwest by late on the 16th resulted in higher snowfall totals shifting to areas west of I-75 with northwest flow lake enhancement taking hold. 48 hour snowfall totals on the order of 1-2 feet by the 17th. Poor conditions due to the snow resulted in cancellation of some events in northeast Lower Michigan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer near Mancelona reported 9.8 inches of fluffy snow in 24hrs ending on the 17th at 8am. Storm total over 48hrs was 14.8 inches. Snow depth of 35inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Official snowfall observer in Alpena reported 8.1inches in 24 hrs by the morning of the 16th. Storm total of 13.5 inches by the morning of the 17th. Calendar day snowfall total of 7.7inches on the 16th broke the daily snowfall record for Alpena...previous record for the calendar day of the 16th was 6.7inches set in 2006.

A large annual snowmobile race at Lincoln, MI was postponed to the following weekend due to whiteout conditions with strengthening winds and snowfall in excess of a foot by midday on the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system crossed the area on the 28th. Warm advection on the front side of this system resulted in a hefty swath of snow across the eastern UP; south of this, snow mixed with and changed over to rain through the day as temperatures rose into the 50s across the southern half of the area. Breezy conditions accompanied this system, and a rapid drop in temperatures occurred as a cold front swept across the area...with lake effect returning on the backside of the system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer on the southern side of Drummond Island reported heavy wet snow and drifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass with temperatures well into the 90s and dewpoints in the 70s resulted in heat index values reaching 100 to 110F on the 22nd. The NWS office in Negaunee set a new daily record high of 93F, breaking the previous record of 90F set in 1995. Cooling centers were opened due to the heat.

A large willow tree at 3 to 4 feet in diameter at the base took down additional powerlines, blocking N Frenchtown Road. Time estimated by broadcast media who forwarded the report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system developed over the Plains, resulting in a warm day (unseasonably warm highs in the 60s) across the area. Enough instability was present, combined with the presence of a warm front, to allow for thunderstorm development in the afternoon and evening hours on the 14th across the eastern UP and Tip of the Mitt. Environment favored and produced accumulating hail, generally half inch to three-quarter inch in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of 1 diameter hail on Neebish Island.

A cold front pushed into the area during the afternoon and evening hours, interacting with  decent shear and MLCAPE to allow for numerous thunderstorm development. Pockets of damaging winds occurred along with a total of six confirmed tornadoes (three in far southern Lower Michigan and three in northern Indiana).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ground and aerial surveys confirm a weak tornado began near the intersection of Olnhausen and Shimmel Rd where over a dozen pine trees were snapped and several others were uprooted. The tornado proceeded quickly northeast removing an empty grain bin and a portion of a pole barn roof west of the intersection of Fawn River and Stubey roads. The grain bin was thrown into power lines along Fawn River Road, possibly aiding in the damage to several snapped power poles. Spans of a center pivot were rolled once. The tornado then continued across open fields to west of Balk Rd and US 12 where trees were blown down onto power lines. A piece of metal from the barn roof was wrapped around a pole several feet off the ground. Another center pivot was damaged northeast of this intersection. The tornado then dissipated shortly thereafter.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed in a field west of M62 and impacted several outbuildings near the intersection of M62 and Pine Lake Street. One outbuilding lost a majority of its metal roof, with debris lofted across the street. The tornado then continued northeast, reaching peak intensity between M62 and Oil City Road. Several groves of trees were snapped or uprooted where peak wind speeds reached 90 mph. The tornado then crossed Oil City Road, snapping trees near the intersection of Jefferson Center Street and M62. Several center pivots were also flipped near that intersection. The tornado then dissipated north of Jefferson Center Street. The tornado was on the ground for 2 minutes and had a peak width of 200 yards.

Pockets of damaging winds occurred along with a total of six confirmed tornadoes (three in far southern Lower Michigan and three in northern Indiana).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pictures of a barn that sustained damage at Bradt and W Main St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season squall line developed over Illinois on the afternoon of March 30, 2025, then tracked northeast across southern Lower Michigan during the late afternoon and evening hours. This line of storms packed a punch of severe to significant winds over much of the state with wind gusts measured between 60 and 70 mph. Pockets of locally higher gusts were also observed, including a gust of 96 mph at the Jackson County Airport. Two weak, short lived tornadoes also developed. There were numerous reports of uprooted trees and property damage, and at least 35,000 customers were left without power according to local news reports. Parts of Southeast Michigan that were particularly hard hit were within Shiawassee, Genesee, Saginaw, Washtenaw, and Wayne Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees reported down on houses.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bands of heavy lake effect snow developed during the early morning hours of February 20th, especially between 5 am and 10 am EST in far northeast Berrien County. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: West and west-northwest wind lake effect snow belts converged over the Keweenaw Peninsula on the 10th and 11th, resulting in heavy snowfall, travel impacts, and school delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CO-OP observer KSGM4 reported an event total snowfall of 14 inches, but no more than 10 per 24 hours. The CO-OP observer QNCM4 Quincy Hill reported an event total snowfall of 10.1 inches with no more than 6.9 per 24 hours. The CO-OP observer PDLM4 reported an event total snowfall of 14 inches but no more than 9.7 inches per 24 hours. The CoCoRaHS observer MI-HG-1 Houghton 1.6 ESE reported an event total snowfall of 8.5 inches. The CoCoRaHS observer MI-HG-21 Atlantic Mine reported an event total snowfall of 8 inches. The CoCoRaHS observer MI-HG-24 Hancock 0.1 ENE reported an event total snowfall of 6.8 inches. The CoCoRaHS observer MI-HS-26 Twin Lakes 0.2 SW reported an event total snowfall of 9.4 inches. A public observation from Houghton reported 9.3 inches. Houghton-Keweenaw County Schools delayed the start of school on the 10th and 11th. Key impacts: travel delays, school delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous low passing through northern Ontario brought rain to the UP in the early morning hours of the 24th. However, enough cool air was present at the surface to induce freezing rain for Marquette County, making the Monday morning commute challenging and causing school closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The official NWS observation in Negaunee Township was 0.05 inches of ice. Staff and family reported multiple difficult challenges to travel including slick parking lots, sidewalks, and non-highway roads. A number of school districts closed for the day. Key impacts: travel delays, school closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds and snow resulted in a significant snow storm across portions of west Michigan from January 21-23.  Travel impacts and closures were noted, especially in Mason, Oceana, Lake, Newaygo, Muskegon and Ottawa Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread 8 to 10 inches of snow were reported in Ottawa County with wind gusts over 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly deepening surface low pressure system crossed just north of Lake Superior on the 6th, dragging a cold front through the region late in the day. This resulted in strong wind gusts of 45-60mph across much of the area...resulting in blowing and drifting of snow onto major highways. The Mackinac Bridge was briefly closed to all traffic for a couple hours during the evening as wind gusts on the Bridge exceeded 65mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Manistee Harbor buoy, MEEM4, recorded a wind gust to 59mph at 640pm. The Manistee Blacker airport, KMBL, a few miles inland, recorded a gust to 58mph at 723pm. Additionally, a trained spotter reported traffic delays on M-55 in central Manistee county between Manistee and Brethren due to crews removing very large trees from the roadway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of snowfall totals of 7-8 inches near Lincoln, MI.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system crossed the area on the 28th. Warm advection on the front side of this system resulted in a hefty swath of snow across the eastern UP; south of this, snow mixed with and changed over to rain through the day as temperatures rose into the 50s across the southern half of the area. Breezy conditions accompanied this system, and a rapid drop in temperatures occurred as a cold front swept across the area...with lake effect returning on the backside of the system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The official snow observer at Sault Ste. Marie, MI reported 9.1 inches of snow by 11:55pm on the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing surface low crossed the Ohio Valley on the 12th. Moisture wrapped around the north side of this system, combined with east flow lake enhancement, resulted in several inches of snowfall across northeast lower Michigan by the morning of the 13th. Highest totals were focused primarily in Iosco county, where over 7 inches of new snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of at least 7 inches of snow across much of Iosco county from Oscoda to National City and East Tawas, where 7.5 and 7.7 inches of snow, respectively, were reported on the morning of the 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southwest to northeast oriented boundary crossing the Thumb of Michigan served as focus for development of a couple rounds of long-duration snowfall as waves moved up this boundary with time. Northeast flow resulted in lake enhancement across northeast lower Michigan through the event; winds backing to north and eventually northwest by late on the 16th resulted in higher snowfall totals shifting to areas west of I-75 with northwest flow lake enhancement taking hold. 48 hour snowfall totals on the order of 1-2 feet by the 17th. Poor conditions due to the snow resulted in cancellation of some events in northeast Lower Michigan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of snowfall totals of 12-20 inches across Presque Isle county. Lots of drifting snow was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of lake effect snow showers and very strong winds created blizzard conditions in Upper Michigan���s Copper Country Thursday afternoon and evening. Areas of blowing and drifting snow were also noted in central and eastern Upper Michigan. The Houghton County Airport reported 7.5 consecutive hours of blizzard conditions and white out conditions were reported on many roadways. Michigan State Police reported that several cars were reported to be in ditches and advised against travel on US-41. Multiple schools in the impacted areas were released early Thursday and delayed their start Friday morning. Peak wind observed at Houghton County Airport was 61 mph while widespread 40-55 mph winds were reported across the forecast area. Snowfall reports ranged from 2 to 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hours of blizzard conditions were reported during the afternoon and evening of the 6th. Multiple schools in the impacted areas were released early on the 6th and delayed their start on the morning of the 7th. Ontonagon Lakeshore and Silver City MDOT mesonet sites reported frequent gusts above 35 mph from 2 PM to 10 PM. Trout Creek MDOT mesonet site reported frequent wind gusts above 35 mph from 11 AM to 7 PM. Key impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents, school closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: West and west-northwest wind lake effect snow belts converged over the Keweenaw Peninsula on the 10th and 11th, resulting in heavy snowfall, travel impacts, and school delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CoCoRaHS observer MI-KW-3 Ahmeek 0.1 E reported a 12-hour snowfall total of 14.5 inches by 8 AM on the 10th. The event total at this location was 21.5 inches. Houghton-Keweenaw County Schools delayed the start of school on the 10th and 11th. Key impacts: travel delays, school delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking through northwest Ohio and into the Eastern Great Lakes produced 3 to 6 inches of snow across most of southeast Michigan Wednesday (Feb 12) afternoon through early Thursday (Feb 13) morning. Sleet and freezing drizzle was mixed in, along and south of I-94, which limited snowfall amounts. With northeast winds off Lake Huron, higher snow totals were observed over the eastern Thumb region, with localized amounts of 6-10 inches. Snow developed Wednesday afternoon and tapered off by 3 AM Thursday, thus road crews were able to clear much of the main roads for the Thursday morning commute. The snow also contained less water content than normal, thus was easier to shovel as well. Heaviest snowfall reports: 10.5 inches in Lexington, 10.0 inches in Deckerville, 7.2 inches in Lapeer,  and 6.5 inches in Columbus.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jeddo reported 7 inches in 12 hours. Kimball 6 inches, Lakeport 6.3 inches, and Columbus 6.5 inches.

The Mackinac Bridge was briefly closed to all traffic for a couple hours during the evening as wind gusts on the Bridge exceeded 65mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind equipment at the Frankfort Light reported multiple gusts greater than 50mph starting as early as 400pm. Highest recorded gust was 61mph at 547pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked through northern Ohio and produced mostly light snow (under 4 inches) over the majority of southeast Michigan. However, northeast winds off Lake Huron lead to some enhancement over Huron County, where 5 to 8 inches of snow fell in a 12-15 hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Port Austin reported 8 inches of snow, with 6.8 inches at Harbor beach, and 6.4 inches at Bad Axe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass settling over the region produced some heavy lake effect snow and extreme cold conditions across much of the UP from the 15th through the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The official observation at the NWS office in Negaunee Township from 7 PM on the 15th to 1 PM on the 16th was 14 inches and counting. Other public reports included 13 inches by 8:30 AM on the 16th 4 miles northeast of Negaunee, 14.5 inches 5 miles north of Negaunee by 10 AM on the 16th, 18 inches 8 miles north of Negaunee by 1 PM on the 16th, and 12 inches estimated 9 miles south-southeast of Big Bay by 7 PM on the 16th. Key impacts: travel delays, road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass settling over the region produced some heavy lake effect snow and extreme cold conditions across much of the UP from the 15th through the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at the Delta County Airport (KESC) reported a wind chill of -21 degrees at 8 AM on the 17th. The mesonet station AW433 Rock reported a wind chill of -20 degrees 9 AM on the 17th. The Delta County Airport AWOS reported a wind chill of -24 degrees the morning of the 18th. The mesonet stations AW433 Rock, MC036 Trenary MDOT, and 7361D Garden all reported wind chills of -20 degrees between 6 AM and 9 AM on the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of heavy lake effect snow produced localized amounts up to a foot in portions of southwest Michigan during the morning hours of February 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bands of heavy lake effect snow developed during the early morning hours of February 20th, especially between 1 am and 6 am EST in and around the Buchanan and Niles area. Snowfall rates of 2��� per hour and near zero visibilities occurred with this heavy snow band. The lake effect snow shower activity did become less organized thereafter, though winds did pick up with blowing and drifting snow observed. School closings and accidents were reported in the region due to the significant snow. Snow accumulations across southern Berrien county were wide ranging, generally between 3 and 12 inches. Niles reported 11.5 inches of snow, with 10.6��� 1 mile east-southeast of Buchanan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass settling over the region produced some heavy lake effect snow and extreme cold conditions across much of the UP from the 15th through the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS station at the Gogebic-Iron County Airport (KIWD) reported a wind chill of -29 at 8 AM on the 18th. THe MDOT Mesonet station at Wakefield reported a wind chill of -25 at 9:30 AM on the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass settling over the region produced some heavy lake effect snow and extreme cold conditions across much of the UP from the 15th through the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station DVI04715 Iron River reported a wind chill of -25 at 5 AM on the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass settling over the region produced some heavy lake effect snow and extreme cold conditions across much of the UP from the 15th through the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station OJIM4 Ojibway - Isle Royale reported a wind chill of -26 degrees at 8:30 AM on the 17th and a wind chill of -29 degrees at 7:30 AM on the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass settling over the region produced some heavy lake effect snow and extreme cold conditions across much of the UP from the 15th through the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS station at the Marquette Sawyer Regional Airport (KSAW) reported a wind chill of -26 degrees at 2 AM on the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass settling over the region produced some heavy lake effect snow and extreme cold conditions across much of the UP from the 15th through the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS station at the Menominee Regional Airport reported a wind chill of -20 degrees at 8 AM the morning of the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass settling over the region produced some heavy lake effect snow and extreme cold conditions across much of the UP from the 15th through the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of lake effect snow occurred between the 16th and the 20th. The most severe conditions occurred on the 16th and 17th when the combination of heavy snow and winds gusting to 40 mph resulted in limited visibility and a road closure on M-28 between Shingleton and Seney. Munising received around 12 inches of snow from the afternoon of the 16th through the morning of the 18th. Key impacts: transportation delays, road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly deepening surface low pressure system crossed just north of Lake Superior on the 6th, dragging a cold front through the region late in the day. This resulted in strong wind gusts of 45-60mph across much of the area...resulting in blowing and drifting of snow onto major highways. The Mackinac Bridge was briefly closed to all traffic for a couple hours during the evening as wind gusts on the Bridge exceeded 65mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Houghton Lake Roscommon County Airport, KHTL, recorded a gust to 61mph at 636pm.

The most severe conditions occurred on the 16th and 17th when the combination of heavy snow and winds gusting to 40 mph resulted in limited visibility and a road closure on M-28 between Shingleton and Seney. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front draped across the region became the focus for an area of widespread synoptic snowfall that developed early in the day on the 3rd. Snowfall totals generally ranged from 4-6 inches, with highest totals largely along the M-72 corridor, though totals as high as 9 inches ranged as far north as Lewiston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports of 8-10 inches across northwestern Oscoda county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and deep low pressure system (for mid-May standards) over the Upper Mississippi River Valley spawned severe thunderstorms, including tornadoes. An intense line of thunderstorms tracked across Lake Michigan during the evening of May 15th. This line produced localized wind gusts of 55-75 MPH over southwest lower Michigan, along with several large hail reports (up to 1.75 inches) and several weak tornadoes. As the line moved into southeast Michigan just before midnight, the moderate instability from the daytime heating (max temps reached into lower 80s) began to wane, and the air mass near the surface began to stabilize. This caused the storms to slowly weaken as they tracked through southeast Michigan during the early morning hours of Friday (May 16th). Despite this, four tornadoes were able to develop across parts of Saginaw, Genesee, and Livingston counties, with isolated to scattered severe thunderstorms across the rest of the area. Over all, most of the Detroit Metro area was spared and saw limited wind damage, as DTE energy outages peaked just under 10,000 customers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree limbs repored down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported a large tree down at US-45 and Himanka Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A unique event impacted the region on Friday May 16th.  Showers and thunderstorms developed across central Illinois and tracked into northern Indiana. Rain cooled downdrafts in a dry environment allowed strong gusty winds of 40 to 70 mph to spread out in advance of the precipitation. These strong winds resulted in numerous reports of tree damage and power outages. In addition, these strong winds preceding the rain led dust and dirt being kicked up into the atmosphere with limited visibility at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many trees down in a small area near the corner of Red Arrow Highway.

Estimated peak winds of 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and deep low pressure system (for mid-May standards) over the Upper Mississippi River Valley spawned severe thunderstorms, including tornadoes. An intense line of thunderstorms tracked across Lake Michigan during the evening of May 15th. This line produced localized wind gusts of 55-75 MPH over southwest lower Michigan, along with several large hail reports (up to 1.75 inches) and several weak tornadoes. As the line moved into southeast Michigan just before midnight, the moderate instability from the daytime heating (max temps reached into lower 80s) began to wane, and the air mass near the surface began to stabilize. This caused the storms to slowly weaken as they tracked through southeast Michigan during the early morning hours of Friday (May 16th). Despite this, four tornadoes were able to develop across parts of Saginaw, Genesee, and Livingston counties, with isolated to scattered severe thunderstorms across the rest of the area. Over all, most of the Detroit Metro area was spared and saw limited wind damage, as DTE energy outages peaked just under 10,000 customers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree reported blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winter storm lifted through the Great Lakes Monday March 4 through Thursday morning. This produced freezing rain, rain, and then widespread heavy, wet snow, strong winds, and blizzard conditions. Widespread 6 to 24 inches of snow was observed across Upper Michigan as well as 35 to 50 mph winds. Highest storm total snow reported was 24 inches 2 miles from WFO Marquette and the highest wind observed was 62 mph at the Marquette Coast Guard Station. WFO Marquette's storm total snow was 22.7 inches. The office also set records for daily maximum QPF at 2.55 inches and daily snowfall 20.8 inches for March 5th. Blizzard conditions were observed for nearly 12 hours at Marquette Sawyer Regional Airport (KSAW). Widespread impacts occurred including: all flights canceled at KSAW, a local TV station canceled their evening broadcast because staff could not get into their station; schools, businesses, ski resorts, churches, public services, and local government buildings were closed Wednesday and Thursday; multiple minor traffic incidents also occurred, including jack-knifed 18 wheelers that resulted in temporary road closures; approximately 5000 power outages occurred, but were resolved quickly. Multiple police and local governments were advising people to stay off the roads due to impossible road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple CO-OP and CoCoRaHS observers reported between 9 and 13 inches of snow, with the highest total being recorded at the MI-IR-3 CoCoRaHS with 12.9 inches by 8 AM on the 6th. Key impacts: school closures, travel delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long duration and very complex series of impactful marine, winter, and convective events took place across Upper Michigan beginning Friday March 28 and persisted into early Monday morning, March 31. The event developed initially as a glaze of ice from freezing drizzle Friday morning, then evolved into a convective event which produced widespread freezing rain and sleet thunderstorms, mixed with small hail. This then became heavy snow in the Keweenaw Peninsula and widespread sleet and freezing rain Friday night before a break was observed Saturday. The next wave moved in Saturday evening and again produced widespread freezing rain, sleet, and some snow. Finally, the last wave moved in Sunday into Sunday night, which produced widespread freezing rain, sleet, and heavy snow. Friday reports included widespread 0.5-1��� of liquid equivalent precipitation in the wintry mix; Saturday, 2 to 8 inches of snow and 0.5-1��� of liquid was observed across the forecast area; and Sunday, 4 to 12 inches was observed across most of the forecast area, except in and around Marquette, which observed 12-20 inches. WFO Marquette observed 18.9 inches of snow and 2.74 of liquid on March 30th, breaking both previous records. Freezing rain amounts of 0.1-0.5 inches were observed in multiple areas during the event. An estimated 75,000 people and many businesses lost power or were closed, and multiple roads were deemed impassible from ice or downed power lines, prompting multiple road closures and at least two 911 centers to advise against travel. Multiple businesses, schools, churches, and local government buildings were closed during this event. Ad-hoc warming shelters were established in various churches in heavily hit areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of wintry precipitation occurred from the evening of the 28th through the evening of the 30th. First, a round of thunderstorms and sleet during the evening of the 28th resulted in slippery conditions. Then, heavy snow developed on the 30th, accumulating 4 to 16 inches, heaviest in the Ironwood area. Key results: travel delays, school and business closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure tracking from northwest Ontario to the eastern UP through the day on February 28th resulted in high winds, brief moderate to heavy snow, and whiteout conditions over portions of the UP. This was a long duration event that impacted the Friday morning commute with widespread 1-3 inches. During the day, rain mixed in with snow in some locations. By the afternoon and evening commute, plummeting temperatures resulted in a flash freeze while very strong winds and moderate to heavy snow resulted in very low visibilities. Highest snow amounts for the event spanned most of Upper Michigan's west, central, and east with amounts ranging from 3 to 11 inches. The same areas observed widespread 40 to 60 mph winds in the afternoon and evening hours. These conditions resulted impassable road conditions in the city of Houghton, occasional white outs in Negaunee, icy road conditions in Marquette, an 11 hour closure of M-28 between Munising and Harvey, multiple school delays and early releases, afternoon and evening sporting event postponements, and power outages in Keweenaw and Dickinson County. The event continued into the morning hours of March 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow and high winds resulted in blizzard conditions during the afternoon and evening of February 28th. The snow wound down early on March 1st. Key impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents, road closures, scattered power outages, school and event cancellations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winter storm lifted through the Great Lakes Monday March 4 through Thursday morning. This produced freezing rain, rain, and then widespread heavy, wet snow, strong winds, and blizzard conditions. Widespread 6 to 24 inches of snow was observed across Upper Michigan as well as 35 to 50 mph winds. Highest storm total snow reported was 24 inches 2 miles from WFO Marquette and the highest wind observed was 62 mph at the Marquette Coast Guard Station. WFO Marquette's storm total snow was 22.7 inches. The office also set records for daily maximum QPF at 2.55 inches and daily snowfall 20.8 inches for March 5th. Blizzard conditions were observed for nearly 12 hours at Marquette Sawyer Regional Airport (KSAW). Widespread impacts occurred including: all flights canceled at KSAW, a local TV station canceled their evening broadcast because staff could not get into their station; schools, businesses, ski resorts, churches, public services, and local government buildings were closed Wednesday and Thursday; multiple minor traffic incidents also occurred, including jack-knifed 18 wheelers that resulted in temporary road closures; approximately 5000 power outages occurred, but were resolved quickly. Multiple police and local governments were advising people to stay off the roads due to impossible road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Houghton County Memorial Airport ASOS KCMX reported 1/4 mile visibility or lower from 1215 to 1908EST on the 5th, with wind gusts ranging from 35 to 50 mph. Winds at the GLOS Weather Station PCLM4 Portage Canal were above 35 mph from about 1030 to around 2100EST. Peak winds at KCMX were 51 mph at 1844EST. Storm total snowfall around the zone ranged from 10 to 20 inches, highest at the CoCoRaHS site MI-HG-26 Twin Lakes of 20.3 inches. Key impacts: Vehicle accidents, travel delays, business closures, government building closures, church closures, power outages, flight cancellations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long duration and very complex series of impactful marine, winter, and convective events took place across Upper Michigan beginning Friday March 28 and persisted into early Monday morning, March 31. The event developed initially as a glaze of ice from freezing drizzle Friday morning, then evolved into a convective event which produced widespread freezing rain and sleet thunderstorms, mixed with small hail. This then became heavy snow in the Keweenaw Peninsula and widespread sleet and freezing rain Friday night before a break was observed Saturday. The next wave moved in Saturday evening and again produced widespread freezing rain, sleet, and some snow. Finally, the last wave moved in Sunday into Sunday night, which produced widespread freezing rain, sleet, and heavy snow. Friday reports included widespread 0.5-1��� of liquid equivalent precipitation in the wintry mix; Saturday, 2 to 8 inches of snow and 0.5-1��� of liquid was observed across the forecast area; and Sunday, 4 to 12 inches was observed across most of the forecast area, except in and around Marquette, which observed 12-20 inches. WFO Marquette observed 18.9 inches of snow and 2.74 of liquid on March 30th, breaking both previous records. Freezing rain amounts of 0.1-0.5 inches were observed in multiple areas during the event. An estimated 75,000 people and many businesses lost power or were closed, and multiple roads were deemed impassible from ice or downed power lines, prompting multiple road closures and at least two 911 centers to advise against travel. Multiple businesses, schools, churches, and local government buildings were closed during this event. Ad-hoc warming shelters were established in various churches in heavily hit areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A final round of freezing rain and sleet turned to snow on the 30th, with up to 6 inches of snowfall before it ended early on the 31st. The additional snow exacerbated travel impacts from the earlier freezing rain. Key impacts: travel delays, school and business closures.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Part of a school gym roof blown off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure tracking from northwest Ontario to the eastern UP through the day on February 28th resulted in high winds, brief moderate to heavy snow, and whiteout conditions over portions of the UP. This was a long duration event that impacted the Friday morning commute with widespread 1-3 inches. During the day, rain mixed in with snow in some locations. By the afternoon and evening commute, plummeting temperatures resulted in a flash freeze while very strong winds and moderate to heavy snow resulted in very low visibilities. Highest snow amounts for the event spanned most of Upper Michigan's west, central, and east with amounts ranging from 3 to 11 inches. The same areas observed widespread 40 to 60 mph winds in the afternoon and evening hours. These conditions resulted impassable road conditions in the city of Houghton, occasional white outs in Negaunee, icy road conditions in Marquette, an 11 hour closure of M-28 between Munising and Harvey, multiple school delays and early releases, afternoon and evening sporting event postponements, and power outages in Keweenaw and Dickinson County. The event continued into the morning hours of March 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow and high winds resulted in blizzard conditions from around 1500 to 2000 EST on February 28th. Winds gusted as high as 51 mph at Houghton County Memorial Airport, and 3-11 inches of snow occurred. A flash freeze resulted in roadways becoming impassible in the Houghton/Hancock area. The snow wound down early on March 1. Key impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents, road closures, scattered power outages, school and event cancellations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season squall line developed over Illinois on the afternoon of March 30, 2025, then tracked northeast across southern Lower Michigan during the late afternoon and evening hours. This line of storms packed a punch of severe to significant winds over much of the state with wind gusts measured between 60 and 70 mph. Pockets of locally higher gusts were also observed, including a gust of 96 mph at the Jackson County Airport. Two weak, short lived tornadoes also developed. There were numerous reports of uprooted trees and property damage, and at least 35,000 customers were left without power according to local news reports. Parts of Southeast Michigan that were particularly hard hit were within Shiawassee, Genesee, Saginaw, Washtenaw, and Wayne Counties.

Friday reports included widespread 0.5-1��� of liquid equivalent precipitation in the wintry mix; Saturday, 2 to 8 inches of snow and 0.5-1��� of liquid was observed across the forecast area; and Sunday, 4 to 12 inches was observed across most of the forecast area, except in and around Marquette, which observed 12-20 inches. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure tracking from northwest Ontario to the eastern UP through the day on February 28th resulted in high winds, brief moderate to heavy snow, and whiteout conditions over portions of the UP. This was a long duration event that impacted the Friday morning commute with widespread 1-3 inches. During the day, rain mixed in with snow in some locations. By the afternoon and evening commute, plummeting temperatures resulted in a flash freeze while very strong winds and moderate to heavy snow resulted in very low visibilities. Highest snow amounts for the event spanned most of Upper Michigan's west, central, and east with amounts ranging from 3 to 11 inches. The same areas observed widespread 40 to 60 mph winds in the afternoon and evening hours. These conditions resulted impassable road conditions in the city of Houghton, occasional white outs in Negaunee, icy road conditions in Marquette, an 11 hour closure of M-28 between Munising and Harvey, multiple school delays and early releases, afternoon and evening sporting event postponements, and power outages in Keweenaw and Dickinson County. The event continued into the morning hours of March 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow and high winds resulted in blizzard conditions during the afternoon and evening of February 28th. Winds gusted as high as 59 mph at Munising, and 3-11 inches of snow occurred. M-28 was closed between Harvey and Munising for 11 hours starting at 2100 EST on the 28th. The snow wound down early on March 1st. Key impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents, road closures, scattered power outages, school and event cancellations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winter storm lifted through the Great Lakes Monday March 4 through Thursday morning. This produced freezing rain, rain, and then widespread heavy, wet snow, strong winds, and blizzard conditions. Widespread 6 to 24 inches of snow was observed across Upper Michigan as well as 35 to 50 mph winds. Highest storm total snow reported was 24 inches 2 miles from WFO Marquette and the highest wind observed was 62 mph at the Marquette Coast Guard Station. WFO Marquette's storm total snow was 22.7 inches. The office also set records for daily maximum QPF at 2.55 inches and daily snowfall 20.8 inches for March 5th. Blizzard conditions were observed for nearly 12 hours at Marquette Sawyer Regional Airport (KSAW). Widespread impacts occurred including: all flights canceled at KSAW, a local TV station canceled their evening broadcast because staff could not get into their station; schools, businesses, ski resorts, churches, public services, and local government buildings were closed Wednesday and Thursday; multiple minor traffic incidents also occurred, including jack-knifed 18 wheelers that resulted in temporary road closures; approximately 5000 power outages occurred, but were resolved quickly. Multiple police and local governments were advising people to stay off the roads due to impossible road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and heavy snow brought blizzard conditions to the region on the 5th. Total snowfall ranged from around 8 to 20 inches. Key impacts: school, church, and building closures, travel delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season squall line developed over Illinois on the afternoon of March 30, 2025, then tracked northeast across southern Lower Michigan during the late afternoon and evening hours. This line of storms packed a punch of severe to significant winds over much of the state with wind gusts measured between 60 and 70 mph. Pockets of locally higher gusts were also observed, including a gust of 96 mph at the Jackson County Airport. Two weak, short lived tornadoes also developed. There were numerous reports of uprooted trees and property damage, and at least 35,000 customers were left without power according to local news reports. Parts of Southeast Michigan that were particularly hard hit were within Shiawassee, Genesee, Saginaw, Washtenaw, and Wayne Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Western side of barn sustained significant damage. Loss of roofing material and exterior walls. A nearby home also sustained roof damage when a tree fell on the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dryline across Illinois progressed east during the late afternoon and evening hours allowing for convection to overcome limited capping. As these storms progressed east, they encountered an unstable airmass near/south of a warm front with some storms producing localized damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at the Three Rivers airport (KHAI) recorded a 59 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and deep low pressure system (for mid-May standards) over the Upper Mississippi River Valley spawned severe thunderstorms, including tornadoes. An intense line of thunderstorms tracked across Lake Michigan during the evening of May 15th. This line produced localized wind gusts of 55-75 MPH over southwest lower Michigan, along with several large hail reports (up to 1.75 inches) and several weak tornadoes. As the line moved into southeast Michigan just before midnight, the moderate instability from the daytime heating (max temps reached into lower 80s) began to wane, and the air mass near the surface began to stabilize. This caused the storms to slowly weaken as they tracked through southeast Michigan during the early morning hours of Friday (May 16th). Despite this, four tornadoes were able to develop across parts of Saginaw, Genesee, and Livingston counties, with isolated to scattered severe thunderstorms across the rest of the area. Over all, most of the Detroit Metro area was spared and saw limited wind damage, as DTE energy outages peaked just under 10,000 customers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dryline across Illinois progressed east during the late afternoon and evening hours allowing for convection to overcome limited capping. As these storms progressed east, they encountered an unstable airmass near/south of a warm front with some storms producing localized damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Benton Harbor airport (KBEH) recorded a 61 mph wind gust.

There it tracked northeast with the peak  damage near Campbell Lake where houses sustained roof damage. The track continued northeast crossing I-96 around Hastings Road|before ending just south of Saranac. The tornado began in Kent County and ended in Ionia County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms around the Keweenaw Bay produced impactful flash flooding in Baraga County on the morning of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A localized area of up to 4.2 inches of rainfall fell (per a public source in Pequaming) in a few hours between about 12:30 AM through about 5 AM, with the Baraga and L'Anse CWOP stations recording rain rates of a quarter to half inch of rain per 15 minutes and various stations recorded 1-2+ inch per hour rates. This sudden rainfall caused road overtopping and washouts in the communities of Keweenaw Bay, Baraga, L'Anse, and Pequaming as well as rural surrounding areas. Bus routes were rerouted and beaches were closed due to wastewater backups causing E. Coli and Fecal Coliform contamination. Many basements and private drives were flooded as well. Local rivers such as the Silver River reported up to a 5 foot increase in only a few hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in flooding along the Anna River late Monday night into early Tuesday morning. Driveway bridges and a private road were damaged or washed out along M-94, and railroad tracks were washed out between Munising Junction and Munising. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere was primed for severe weather during the afternoon and evening hours of April 2nd. 0-1 km SRH values of 300 to 500 m2/s2, 60 to 70 kts of shear and  MLCAPE of around 1000 J/KG set the stage for numerous thunderstorms. Damage reports were not as widespread, but those that occurred tended to be in swaths related to cold pools from bowing segments or tornadoes. Eight tornadoes (three in northern Indiana, one which crossed from Indiana into Lower Michigan, one in lower Michigan and three in northwestern Ohio). A total of five injuries were reported (three in NW Ohio and two in N Indiana).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage survey from the National Weather Service along with Steuben County EMA found damage consistent with a tornado that touched down on the south side of Orland, IN. The tornado destroyed an old, small barn on the south side, before uprooting and snapping several trees and a communications pole in east Orland. The tornado damaged the roof of an apartment building, including ripping up the wood sheathing on the south end of the building. Non-conclusive, likely rear flank downdraft winds severely damaged a large barn at N 900 W and W State Rd 120 and a set of trees just off to the east. Across the street, a dozen or so trees were uprooted or snapped in a cemetery. Tree damage was also noted in the adjacent tree line. The tornado reached max width at the border of IN/MI where a large swath of significant tree damage occurred to a piece of property owned by the Indiana Toll Road. A semi-truck was reported to be blown over on the Toll Rd. The tornado crossed into Michigan with intermittent light structural and tree damage occurring near East Gilead and extending northeastward. The tornado reached maximum intensity of 105 mph (EF-1) when it struck a farm on E Pearl Road, tearing roofs off large silos, destroying a section of barn, driving 2x4 into the nearby home, and destroying another out building to the west.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt flooding caused damage to infrastructure in Marquette County on the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spring snowmelt and flooding have forced the DNR to the temporary emergency closure of Arfelin Lake boating access site in Marquette County until road repairs can be made. Culvert and the dirt access road have failed and water is moving swiftly. A pickup truck became stuck when a portion of the road collapsed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 35 year old man drowned in Lake Huron after being swept into the lake from a strong river outlet current. The Huron County Sherrif's Office noted that a couple had been walking in 3 to 4 feet of water near the mouth of the Pinnebog River when they stepped off into murky water where ther river bed had eroded due to the fast moving river current. The outlet current quick swept the 35 year old man into Lake Huron, while the other individual was able to escape back to shore. Heavy rain the morning off the drowning contributed to the strong river outlet current, which opens up and flows into Lake Huron, acting similiarly to a rip current.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An cluster of slow moving, training thundestorms produced torrential rainfall over northern portions of Oakland County on the evening of April 25th. This resulted in a localized area of 2 to 3 inches of rain over Oakland County. The heaviest rain fall between approximtely 4:30pm and 7pm EDT, with light rain continuing through approximately 9pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports from the public on social media showing water flowing over several roads from Lake Orion to east of Lakeville, and areas south into the Oakland township. One video in particular shows water flowing over 32 Mile east of Lakeville, with active runoff flowing towards and over the road from the higher terrain south of 32 Mile. Oakland Township also had a number of road closures due to flooded roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Morning snow, light freezing rain, and some sleet transitioned to rain showers and thunderstorms by midday on April 2nd. An anomalously warm and humid low-level air mass moved into Lower Michigan while the surface warm front lagged well to the south, over the Ohio Valley. Numerous rounds of repeated rainfall during the day and overnight led to several areas across Metro Detroit receiving between 2 and 3 of rainfall within a 24 hour period. This led to multiple reports of flooded roadways, stranded vehicles, basement flooding, and water damage to businesses. In addition to flooding, hail in excess of 1 inch in diameter was reported over parts of Huron county during the early evening.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Within the same parent cell as the Orland tornado, a NWS survey found a secondary tornado occurred in Branch County Michigan. Tree damage was first noted between W Southern Rd and Booth Rd. The tornado traveled northeast and then northerly, causing damage to multiple Amish buildings, many of which had already been repaired and debris cleaned up. Minor roof damage was noted to a barn on Cranson Rd.  The tornado caused the most significant damage on W Pearl Rd west of Bethel where numerous large healthy trees were snapped or uprooted. Damage was also noted to a home, but repairs were completed prior to the survey. Maximum wind speeds were estimated at up to 100 mph based on the tree and limited structure damage that could be assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season squall line developed over Illinois on the afternoon of March 30, 2025, then tracked northeast across southern Lower Michigan during the late afternoon and evening hours. This line of storms packed a punch of severe to significant winds over much of the state with wind gusts measured between 60 and 70 mph. Pockets of locally higher gusts were also observed, including a gust of 96 mph at the Jackson County Airport. Two weak, short lived tornadoes also developed. There were numerous reports of uprooted trees and property damage, and at least 35,000 customers were left without power according to local news reports. Parts of Southeast Michigan that were particularly hard hit were within Shiawassee, Genesee, Saginaw, Washtenaw, and Wayne Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two foot diameter tree reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, and very moist air mass in the Midwest, combined with a front slowly leaning into the region, resulted in development of upstream convection that, even after decaying earlier in the day, was enough of an atmospheric perturbation to kick off another line of thunderstorms. This line crossed Lake Michigan during the late evening hours on the 23rd. This line resulted in gusty winds and thunderstorm wind damage, particularly across northwest Lower Michigan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and powerlines down on Thompsonville Highway, south of US-31.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe storms moved across the area in the afternoon and evening of Wednesday, June 18th, 2025. Damage consisted of tree damage, scattered reports of power lines down, and some associated roof and siding damage to buildings and homes. Wind gusts were estimated to be between 50-60 mph. There were a few reports of quarter to golf ball size hail in northeast Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of powerlines down on Mosherville Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms impacted portions of Southeast Michigan on June 18, 2025. The first round consisted of sub-severe pop-up thunderstorms during the morning hours. These produced pockets of heavy downpours but otherwise brought some relief to areas that had seen relatively dry conditions in recent days. Showers expanded in coverage across the southern part of the state by late morning to early afternoon as a disturbance aloft ushered in a more organized cluster of thunderstorms. These were also sub-severe, but the presence of environmental vorticity caused a weak tornado to quickly develop as several outflow boundaries collided near Fraser in Macomb County. These storms also produced torrential downpours. Nearly stationary storms dumped 3 to 5 inches of rain over a localized area in central Macomb County where flash flooding occurred early in the afternoon, closing roads and leading to several water rescues. A lull in activity occurred during the mid afternoon before a third round of thunderstorms arrived in the evening as a low pressure system approached from Indiana. This line of storms caused isolated occurrences of wind damage across southern parts of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado started north of Masonic and east of Utica Road and Garfield Road, in the vicinity of General Street. The tornado moved north crossing 14 Mile Road and affected about 15 to 20 homes, with shingles blown off their roofs. The most concentrated damage was in this neighborhood, with a lot of tree damage as well. The most |significant structural damage that was noted was 1 house losing a section of its roof. The tornado continued to move north crossing 15 Mile Road causing additional tree|damage before dissipating just south of the Metro Parkway. Estimated peak winds of 70 MPH with the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure tracking from northwest Ontario to the eastern UP through the day on the 28th resulted in high winds, brief moderate to heavy snow, and whiteout conditions over portions of the UP. This was a long duration event that impacted the Friday morning commute with widespread 1-3 inches. During the day, rain mixed in with snow in some locations. By the afternoon and evening commute, plummeting temperatures resulted in a flash freeze while very strong winds and moderate to heavy snow resulted in very low visibilities. Highest snow amounts for the event spanned most of Upper Michigan's west, central, and east with amounts ranging from 3 to 11 inches. The same areas observed widespread 40 to 60 mph winds in the afternoon and evening hours. These conditions resulted impassable road conditions in the city of Houghton, occasional white outs in Negaunee, icy road conditions in Marquette, an 11 hour closure of M-28 between Munising and Harvey, multiple school delays and early releases, afternoon and evening sporting event postponements, and power outages in Keweenaw and Dickinson County. The event continued into the morning hours of March 1. Key impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents, road closures, scattered power outages, school and event cancellations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS station at the Ford Airport (KIMT) reported a brief northwest wind gust of 53 mph at 4 PM on the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure tracking from northwest Ontario to the eastern UP through the day on the 28th resulted in high winds, brief moderate to heavy snow, and whiteout conditions over portions of the UP. This was a long duration event that impacted the Friday morning commute with widespread 1-3 inches. During the day, rain mixed in with snow in some locations. By the afternoon and evening commute, plummeting temperatures resulted in a flash freeze while very strong winds and moderate to heavy snow resulted in very low visibilities. Highest snow amounts for the event spanned most of Upper Michigan's west, central, and east with amounts ranging from 3 to 11 inches. The same areas observed widespread 40 to 60 mph winds in the afternoon and evening hours. These conditions resulted impassable road conditions in the city of Houghton, occasional white outs in Negaunee, icy road conditions in Marquette, an 11 hour closure of M-28 between Munising and Harvey, multiple school delays and early releases, afternoon and evening sporting event postponements, and power outages in Keweenaw and Dickinson County. The event continued into the morning hours of March 1. Key impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents, road closures, scattered power outages, school and event cancellations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow and high winds resulted in blizzard conditions during the afternoon and evening of the 28th. Winds gusted as high as 58 mph at Copper Harbor, and several inches of snow occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 4 day heat wave ended on June 24th as showers and thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours and a cold front pushed through the region. Over half a dozen thunderstorms become severe, with wind damage being the main hazard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, and very moist air mass in the Midwest, combined with a front slowly leaning into the region, resulted in development of upstream convection that, even after decaying earlier in the day, was enough of an atmospheric perturbation to kick off another line of thunderstorms. This line crossed Lake Michigan during the late evening hours on the 23rd. This line resulted in gusty winds and thunderstorm wind damage, particularly across northwest Lower Michigan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Central dispatch reported significant power outages in the vicinity of Manton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Later in the afternoon on the 24th, a line of storms, associated with the morning storms across the Tip of the Mitt, developed across the southern/southeastern portions of the forecast area, resulting in pockets of tree damage from northeastern Gladwin county and Ogemaw county, eastward to Lake Huron.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media post from Alger Area Firefighter Station 15 to avoid Maple Ridge Rd between Melita Rd and M-33 due to downed trees, in the vicinity of Skidway Lake and Prescott. Additional reports of damage in the Forest Lake and north Clayton Twp/Mills Twp area. Comments also included a photo of a healthy fallen tree on top of a late-model SUV on a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection developed along and north of a warm front in the vicinity of the M-32/M-68 corridors on the evening of the 29th. After an initial round of thunderstorms and gravity-wave-induced gusty winds took place on the evening of the 28th, the boundary stalled over the region. Sustained cold, easterly, low-level flow kept surface temperatures largely at or below freezing near and north of the M-72 across northeast Lower Michigan, as an unusually moist air mass overran the boundary, resulting in nearly 60hrs of freezing drizzle/rain. 3-day liquid/liquid-equivalent rainfall totals in excess of 2-4 inches across much of the northern Lower Peninsula translated to radial ice accumulations in sub-freezing locations of 1/2 to nearly 1 inch by Sunday. This led to devastation of 3 million acres of forest and subsequent home/property damage due to trees collapsing under the weight of the ice. Dozens of Michigan state parks and campgrounds were damaged, many to the point of temporary closures for safety and cleanup. Many maple syrup farms in the region suffered partial or total destruction of more than half their maple trees, as well as to syrup-making equipment. Additionally, extensive damage was done to the power and communications infrastructure of northern Michigan:  multiple TV and FM radio broadcast towers were toppled under the weight of the ice; almost the entire power grid of northern Michigan was crippled, resulting in unprecedented near-complete outages across multiple counties in northeast Lower Michigan, with thousands of line workers coming in from across the nation and internationally from Canada to assist with recovery and repair in damage that has been likened to a Category 5 hurricane, but with bitterly cold temperatures. Over 120,000 people were without power during the peak of the storm. Power did not return for some areas until late in April, nearly 3 weeks after the ice storm had departed. Emergency services, including multiple hospitals, dispatch centers, fire departments, and the National Weather Service office 9miles SSW of Gaylord, MI, were on backup generator power for several days following the ice storm, as first responders and other emergency services personnel worked around the clock in hazardous conditions, sometimes staying at work as shelter-in-place orders were issued for multiple counties due to the danger of falling trees and powerlines along thousands of miles of blocked roads and trails. 12 counties in northern Michigan were put under a state of emergency by the Michigan governor; a federal disaster declaration came several weeks later. The National Guard was deployed to assist with cleanup, recovery, and restoration as miles of primary and secondary roads were blocked for multiple days; several off-road trails and state parks were closed for weeks due to significant tree damage and danger from parts of trees still hanging in the canopy. The Mackinac Bridge closed several times, some for long durations, due to falling ice concerns, further hampering clean-up and recovery efforts as gas shortages spread from northeast Lower Michigan into the eastern UP. Initial estimates of damage and restoration costs related to the ice storm approached $200 million and are likely higher; economic impacts from power outages and gas shortages notwithstanding. Recovery from this once-in-a-generation ice storm in northern Michigan will continue for years to come.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant icing of amounts greater than a quarter inch (radial) resulted in power outages and tree damage on the island; residents were able to ice skate on the streets, though online sales from year-round businesses on the island were impacted by the power outages. Many roads and trails were closed due to tree damage. The National Guard was deployed to Mackinac Island to help with cleanup.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: By 8pm EDT on the 29th, enough ice had accreted that Montmorency County emergency management issued a shelter in place notice due to danger posed by falling trees. Multiple shelters were set up across the county, with local businesses stepping up to help their communities while power companies worked tirelessly to rebuild much of the power and communication infrastructure in the county, destroyed due to the weight of ice and falling trees. Over 90% of people in Montmorency county were without power at the peak of the event. Johannesburg-Lewiston School District was closed for 2 weeks due to the ice storm; returning students remained out of power at home for longer. Significant tree damage noted across the county, including destruction of maple trees and equipment used in the production of maple syrup.

Over 120,000 people were without power during the peak of the storm. Power did not return for some areas until late in April, nearly 3 weeks after the ice storm had departed. Emergency services, including multiple hospitals, dispatch centers, fire departments, and the National Weather Service office 9miles SSW of Gaylord, MI, were on backup generator power for several days following the ice storm, as first responders and other emergency services personnel worked around the clock in hazardous conditions, sometimes staying at work as shelter-in-place orders were issued for multiple counties due to the danger of falling trees and powerlines along thousands of miles of blocked roads and trails. 12 counties in northern Michigan were put under a state of emergency by the Michigan governor; a federal disaster declaration came several weeks later. The National Guard was deployed to assist with cleanup, recovery, and restoration as miles of primary and secondary roads were blocked for multiple days; several off-road trails and state parks were closed for weeks due to significant tree damage and danger from parts of trees still hanging in the canopy. The Mackinac Bridge closed several times, some for long durations, due to falling ice concerns, further hampering clean-up and recovery efforts as gas shortages spread from northeast Lower Michigan into the eastern UP. Initial estimates of damage and restoration costs related to the ice storm approached $200 million and are likely higher; economic impacts from power outages and gas shortages notwithstanding. Recovery from this once-in-a-generation ice storm in northern Michigan will continue for years to come.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid airmass supported the development of thunderstorms. Weak winds aloft limited storm motion speeds leading to some stationary storms that produced flooding. One storm developed right over Detroit Metro Airport, producing 2.68 of rainfall in 2.5 hours which lead to flash flooding. Additional storms near Sterling Heights produced radar estamited 3 inches of rain, which also led to flash flooding.  Isolated severe storms in Bay and Wayne Counties produced wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous downed power lines reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong low pressure system developed as it crosses the Upper Midwest through the day on the 15th, resulting in an area of widespread strong winds crossing the Upper Great Lakes.  This system also carried with it large amounts of smoke from wildfires and dust from recent dust storms over the central and southwestern US that had lofted significant amounts of dust into the atmosphere, resulting in an unusually hazy day for mid March with reduced visibility at the surface, as well as dirty rain -- raindrops that had formed around dust particles, fell, and once evaporated, left behind a layer of dust on surfaces. Temperatures again reached into the 60s and 70s across parts of the area ahead of the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind equipment at the NWS Gaylord, MI 9miles SSW of Gaylord, MI recorded a gust to 64mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated Severe Storms brought gusty winds and small hail to parts of Eaton County on July 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large tree branches down in Eaton Rapids.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convective development along and north of the warm front with some pea to dime size hail in the Straits region early. During the evening hours, this convective activity was intensified by atmospheric gravity waves that allowed for strong downward momentum transport in an otherwise very stable low-level environment that was unfavorable for damaging winds. Scattered power outages prior to icing occurred with these storms, as well as some wind damage. Thunderstorms continued into the night, with precipitation across the M-68/M-32 corridors slowly transitioning from rain to freezing rain with time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree blown down onto home, and a roof blown off a campground building, amid other wind damage reports from the East Jordan area. Trained spotter reported a gust to 52 mph in the Boyne City area, and the AWOS sensor at the Boyne Falls airport reported a gust to 51mph at 21:34. Several power outages were noted in the East Jordan area during the evening and overnight, prior to icing-related outages.

Alpena Power Company lost all power after the substation serving their grid went down for close to 2 days. The ASOS at the Alpena County Regional Airport, due to power outages, was unable to report critical weather/historical weather information for almost 6 days, between March 29th and April 4th. Some farmers ran generators part time between their homes and their barns to keep livestock safe. An Alpena Public Schools agriscience teacher estimated up to 25 percent of students' greenhouse hanging flower baskets were damaged by cold exposure due to lack of power and heat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection developed along and north of a warm front in the vicinity of the M-32/M-68 corridors on the evening of the 29th. After an initial round of thunderstorms and gravity-wave-induced gusty winds took place on the evening of the 28th, the boundary stalled over the region. Sustained cold, easterly, low-level flow kept surface temperatures largely at or below freezing near and north of the M-72 across northeast Lower Michigan, as an unusually moist air mass overran the boundary, resulting in nearly 60hrs of freezing drizzle/rain. 3-day liquid/liquid-equivalent rainfall totals in excess of 2-4 inches across much of the northern Lower Peninsula translated to radial ice accumulations in sub-freezing locations of 1/2 to nearly 1 inch by Sunday. This led to devastation of 3 million acres of forest and subsequent home/property damage due to trees collapsing under the weight of the ice. Dozens of Michigan state parks and campgrounds were damaged, many to the point of temporary closures for safety and cleanup. Many maple syrup farms in the region suffered partial or total destruction of more than half their maple trees, as well as to syrup-making equipment. Additionally, extensive damage was done to the power and communications infrastructure of northern Michigan:  multiple TV and FM radio broadcast towers were toppled under the weight of the ice; almost the entire power grid of northern Michigan was crippled, resulting in unprecedented near-complete outages across multiple counties in northeast Lower Michigan, with thousands of line workers coming in from across the nation and internationally from Canada to assist with recovery and repair in damage that has been likened to a Category 5 hurricane, but with bitterly cold temperatures. Over 120,000 people were without power during the peak of the storm. Power did not return for some areas until late in April, nearly 3 weeks after the ice storm had departed. Emergency services, including multiple hospitals, dispatch centers, fire departments, and the National Weather Service office 9miles SSW of Gaylord, MI, were on backup generator power for several days following the ice storm, as first responders and other emergency services personnel worked around the clock in hazardous conditions, sometimes staying at work as shelter-in-place orders were issued for multiple counties due to the danger of falling trees and powerlines along thousands of miles of blocked roads and trails. 12 counties in northern Michigan were put under a state of emergency by the Michigan governor; a federal disaster declaration came several weeks later. The National Guard was deployed to assist with cleanup, recovery, and restoration as miles of primary and secondary roads were blocked for multiple days; several off-road trails and state parks were closed for weeks due to significant tree damage and danger from parts of trees still hanging in the canopy. The Mackinac Bridge closed several times, some for long durations, due to falling ice concerns, further hampering clean-up and recovery efforts as gas shortages spread from northeast Lower Michigan into the eastern UP. Initial estimates of damage and restoration costs related to the ice storm approached $200 million and are likely higher; economic impacts from power outages and gas shortages notwithstanding. Recovery from this once-in-a-generation ice storm in northern Michigan will continue for years to come.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant ice damage to parts of Beaver Island, resulting in the National Guard and Michigan Department of Natural Resources being deployed to Beaver Island for cleanup and restoration efforts.

While many areas received ice, including along the coast of Little Traverse Bay north of Charlevoix, lower elevation areas did not receive nearly as much ice or damage from the ice storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system developed over the Plains, resulting in a warm day (unseasonably warm highs in the 60s) across the area. Enough instability was present, combined with the presence of a warm front, to allow for thunderstorm development in the afternoon and evening hours on the 14th across the eastern UP and Tip of the Mitt. Environment favored and produced accumulating hail, generally half inch to three-quarter inch in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of accumulating hail up to 0.75inch diameter between Pickford and Barbeau, MI, including report from COOP observer 2NE Pickford.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant icing across the county, resulting in numerous downed trees and powerlines, as well as gas shortages related to power outages at distribution centers in ice storm affected areas, and frequent and prolonged closures of the Mackinac Bridge due to falling ice concerns. Mackinac Bridge employees reported unprecedented amounts of ice buildup on the Bridge cables and towers following the ice storm, which took several days to finally clear off.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: KPLN, Pellston Regional Airport, ASOS reported 1/4 inch of ice accumulation by 2200 LST on the 28th. Closer to Little Traverse Bay, the cooperative observer at North Central Michigan College in Petoskey reported 1/4 inch of radial ice by the morning observation for the 29th; maximum radial ice accumulation at this cooperative observing station was 0.86inch. Other areas of the county received upwards of 3/4 inch of radial ice accumulation, with significant tree and infrastructure damage, including multiple TV and FM radio broadcast towers being toppled due to the weight of the ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifting through Upper Michigan caused thunderstorms which resulted in flooding in Houghton County and wind damage in western and central Upper Michigan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management, Law Enforcement, Broadcast Media, State Department of Transportation, and the Public reported flash flooding on the morning of the 21st. In Chassell, 4,000 yards of material on Massie Rd was washed out and Sadjak Rd was closed due to water over the road. In Houghton, standing water was observed on many roads, at least 2 road washouts occurred, and surface damage including sidewalk damage was observed at Agate St and MacInnes Dr. The City Council approved $150,000 for repairs. In Hubbell, debris was deposited on roads. In Calumet, sewer systems were overwhelmed and backing up into residences. Damage as also observed in Silver Creek.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple downed trees have been observed by law enforcement in the City of Houghton. The public has been observed cleaning up the debris.
