EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of downed trees between Sac Bay and Fayette. Most of Garden Peninsula lost power for around 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure working its way up a boundary led to development of scattered showers and storms across Lower Michigan, which produced mainly heavy rain and some hail as they moved from southwest to northeast through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from storm chaser group's social media page of measured hail in the Manistee area of 1 to 1.5 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably hot and humid air mass beneath a strong upper ridge in place across the Midwest led to the development of storms late on the evening of the 20th across the Northern Plains. These congealed into a thunderstorm complex over the Upper Midwest, tracking eastward along the perimeter of the ridge, into the Upper Great Lakes on the morning of the 21st. A NW-SE oriented broken line of storms developed along a warm advection axis ahead of the initial line of storms; this produced hail up to the size of golf balls across parts of northern Lower Michigan, including the Traverse City metro area. Meanwhile, a strong mesolow developed at the heart of the slowly decaying thunderstorm complex, ultimately driving a meteotsunami across the length of Lake Superior, resulting in significant water level changes of as much as 2-4ft on Whitefish Bay and the Upper St. Mary's River during the morning hours. This resulted in delays in freighter traffic through the Soo Locks, the waterway through which nearly 100 percent of America's domestic iron ore passes. Periodic water level fluctuations up to 2 feet remained present on the eastern end of Lake Superior through the next couple days, and water levels did not settle back down to normal for several days through the end of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of golf ball size hail on I-75 in Cheboygan county. Additional reports of hail up to the size of a quarter near/in the city of Cheboygan, MI between 0850 and 0900.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant water level changes measured by water level equipment at the Point Iroquois, MI Light on the southern shore of Whitefish Bay as a mesolow at the heart of a decaying line of storms crossed the lake. A 3.75-ft change in water level from trough to peak during the morning hours is the largest in 30-years of water level records at Point Iroquois, MI. Water level fluctuations of 18 or more inches continued through the next day; elevated seiche activity was observed in the Whitefish Bay basin through the next several days.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant water level changes measured by water level equipment at Southwest Pier at the mouth of the St. Mary's River shipping channel, upstream of the Soo Locks. An amplitude of 6.67-ft of change from trough to peak is the largest in 20 years of seiche/meteotsunami data at this location. This resulted in delay of marine traffic through the Soo Locks until water level fluctuations had dampened enough for safe navigation of the shipping channel on the upstream end of the Soo Locks. Water level fluctuations were also noted on the Sault Ste. Marie, MI power canal, resulting in impacts to the Cloverland Hydroelectric Plant at the end of the power canal downstream of the Soo Locks. Additional periodic water level fluctuations were noted around Whitefish Bay and the upper St. Mary's River for the next several days until finally settling back down to normal.

Additional reports and photos of similar sized hail in the Traverse City area on social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of training thunderstorms brought flash flooding to portions of Houghton and Ontonagon Counties between 0300 and 0730EST on July 23. Four-hour rainfall totals ranged from two to four inches in the area. The Governor of Michigan declared a state of emergency as a result of the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Old Rink Road was washed out near the South Branch of the Elm River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon hours, producing wind damage and mostly impacting the Detroit Metro Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree limb was observed down.

Multiple people were rescued from rip currents Sunday afternoon. A majority of the rescues happened when the flag changed from yellow to red. Within minutes there were multiple rescues as people were finding themselves in trouble due to the unexpected change in lake conditions.  It was estimated there were 5 rescues all within 5 minutes.|Flags were changed from yellow to red at 4:07 PM EDT on Sunday, July 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five people were rescued in the span of 5 minutes at New Buffalo Beach. They |were rescued around the time that water conditions deteriorated and flags were | changed from yellow to red, which would have been around 4 PM EDT. Lifeguards were able to rescue all 5 people from waves and currents.

Several low-topped supercells developed along a warm front during the afternoon hours of July 26th, 2025, one of which produced a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down west of Maple Grove Road and south of|Richmondville Road. Based on drone footage from the Sanilac County|Sheriff, it was determined that the tornado started along the|southwest edge of a sugar beet field. The tornado then tracked|slightly north moving across an adjacent corn field. As the |tornado moved east, tree damage was noted on either side of Maple |Grove Road. Numerous large tree limbs were snapped and carried 50 |to 100 yards to the east and northeast. The top of a large pine |tree was torn off a nearby tree and impaled a semi- truck. A few |vehicles were picked up by the tornado, moved about 15 to 20 yards|and pivoted by about 180 degrees. A nearby large farm building |sustained damage to the back wall and lost a few roof panels. |These roof panels were found approximately 1000 yards to the east |in a nearby wheat field. Numerous vehicles sustained damage, with |a few blown out windows and a large camper was flipped on its |side. The tornado continued east and moved over mainly farm |fields, resulting in damage to corn and sugar beet crops. A |distinct path, about 50 yards wide, of corn was flattened in a |convergent pattern across these farm fields. The continued path |across sugar beet fields was less discernible on the ground, but |more visible in aerial footage from the Michigan State Police. |After crossing numerous farm fields, the tornado continued east |and impacted another property near Loree Road, where a large |machine shed and small shed were destroyed. Debris was scattered |into the field crossing Loree Road. The tornado continued to move |east, but quickly dissipated after causing some minor tree damage |closer to Richmondville Road. Maximum winds were estimated at 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper trough axis over the Upper Midwest during the day on the 22nd, with various disturbances rotating through the flow. A decaying mesoscale convective vortex tracked from southeastern WI in the morning to northwest lower MI by evening; a regeneration of storms with this disturbance across northwest lower MI in the late afternoon in an environment otherwise marginally favorable for severe weather resulted in storm rotation that stretched to the ground. A short-lived tornado touched down in northwestern Grand Traverse County; another tornado touched down in the vicinity of Torch Lake in Antrim county, which did track over the lake, producing a very photogenic tornadic waterspout and resulting in some damage in the city of Bellaire, MI. The area of storms progressed eastward, producing hail southwest of Gaylord, MI and a photogenic funnel cloud north of Grayling, MI with no known evidence of a touchdown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extremely photogenic tornadic waterspout developed over central Torch Lake and dissipated shortly after making landfall on the eastern shore of Torch Lake north of the mouth of the Clam River. Only minor tree damage was observed. Event was initially reported to NWS Gaylord by a storm chaser watching a Torch Lake area webcam; chaser reported seeing debris lofted in the air on the webcam footage as the waterspout made landfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Troughing crossing the region on the evening of the 1st into the 2nd resulted in southwest flow lake enhanced snowfall off Lake Michigan. Primary areas impacted were from Charlevoix up the coast to the Straits of Mackinac, and into parts of eastern Mackinac County near Cedarville, where as much as 10-15 inches or more of snow fell by the middle of the day on the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of snowfall totals in excess of a foot across northwestern portions of Emmet county, where as much as 17 inches of snow were reported near Pellston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and very humid air mass in place created an unstable environment, which, in the presence of the Lake Huron lake breeze and a subtle stationary boundary stretching from the Keweenaw Peninsula to northern Lake Huron, was able to support development of scattered strong to severe storms across parts of northeast Lower Michigan during the late afternoon on the 7th, resulting in primarily large hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of hailstones up to 2 inches in diameter near El Cajon and Misery Bays on the Lake Huron coast east of Alpena.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong low pressure system crossed northern Michigan during the overnight hours on the 28th into the 29th. Somewhat milder air ahead of the low led to some areas of rain/freezing rain during the overnight hours, before transitioning to snow as much colder air rushed into the region behind a cold front.  Highest snowfall totals ranged from 8-10 inches across NW lower Michigan to nearly 2 feet across western Chippewa and Mackinac counties. Sustained winds of 20-30mph were accompanied by widespread wind gusts of 40-50mph���including some stronger wind gusts to around 60-70mph early in the morning. Synoptic moisture departed through the afternoon and snowfall tapered off to NW lake effect bands through the afternoon as winds slowly diminished.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amount reached 7.5 inches in 12 hrs by 10:00 near Indian River; snowfall total estimates of 8 inches at the cooperative observing site in the city of Cheboygan, and up to 10 inches near Mullett Lake. Cheboygan Airport, KSLH, reported several hours with visibilities at or below 1/4mi, along with falling snow and wind gusts in excess of 35mph. A 55 year old woman was killed in a collision in the village of Tower during these conditions; Michigan State Police reported they did not believe she was wearing her seatbelt. Another man sustained non-life-threatening injuries in the collision.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports of several inches of snow and blizzard conditions on Mackinac Island. Ferry service to the Island was cancelled for the day due to the weather. Blizzard conditions were detected by AWOS visibility sensor reporting a 3hr period of 1/4 mi visibilities at KMCD, Mackinac Island Airport, between 09:30 and 13:00, and strong winds in excess of 35mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure rapidly deepened 22 mb in 24 hours as it tracked northeastward from the mid-Mississippi Valley to Chicago to the Georgian Bay, bringing blizzard conditions to the UP on the 28th and 29th. Rain turned to snow during the afternoon and evening of the 28th with a few inches of heavy, wet snow developing and sticking to trees. As the low moved east, cold air quickly rushed in and heavy snow continued through the early morning hours of the 29th. Winds increased from the north with 40���60 mph wind gusts. The combination of these winds and the snow/ice-laden trees resulted in significant/locally extreme tree damage, especially in the greater Marquette area, where 62% of customers of the local utility lost power. In terms of these impacts, it was one of the most impactful storms in recent memory. Travel conditions became difficult to impossible with heavy blowing and drifting snow and near-zero visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and gusty winds impacted Dickinson county with 8-12 of snowfall. 9 inches of snowfall was reported near Iron Mountain. Key Impacts: Very difficult to impossible travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure rapidly deepened 22 mb in 24 hours as it tracked northeastward from the mid-Mississippi Valley to Chicago to the Georgian Bay, bringing blizzard conditions to the UP on the 28th and 29th. As the low moved east, cold air quickly rushed in and heavy snow continued through the early morning hours of the 29th. Winds increased from the north with 40���60 mph wind gusts. The combination of these winds and the snow/ice-laden trees resulted in significant/locally extreme tree damage, especially in the greater Marquette area, where 62% of customers of the local utility lost power. In terms of these impacts, it was one of the most impactful storms in recent memory. Travel conditions became difficult to impossible with heavy blowing and drifting snow and near-zero visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were blizzard conditions with frequent wind gusts of 40-55 mph and snowfall of 8-12 in Marquette County. The K.I. Sawyer (KSAW) AWOS peak gust was 53 mph before the wind sensor stopped reporting. Widespread tree damage and power outages were reported, particularly near the lakeshore.  Some structures were damaged from trees falling on them. The Marquette Board of Light and Power reported (summarizing) the event rivaled anything in the past 10 to 20 years and the scale of damage, restoration and coordination was extraordinary. Up to 39% of customers were without power.  Some locations took up to 3 days to restore power.  Multiple warming shelters were opened. Road closures included M-28 due to whiteout conditions. Key Impacts: Very difficult to impossible travel, power outages, tree damage, structure damage, road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure rapidly deepened 22 mb in 24 hours as it tracked northeastward from the mid-Mississippi Valley to Chicago to the Georgian Bay, bringing blizzard conditions to the UP on the 28th and 29th. Rain turned to snow during the afternoon and evening of the 28th with a few inches of heavy, wet snow developing and sticking to trees. As the low moved east, cold air quickly rushed in and heavy snow continued through the early morning hours of the 29th. Winds increased from the north with 40���60 mph wind gusts. The combination of these winds and the snow/ice-laden trees resulted in significant/locally extreme tree damage, especially in the greater Marquette area, where 62% of customers of the local utility lost power. In terms of these impacts, it was one of the most impactful storms in recent memory. Travel conditions became difficult to impossible with heavy blowing and drifting snow and near-zero visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were blizzard conditions with frequent wind gusts of 40-50 mph and snowfall of 8-20 in Delta County. The Escanaba ASOS (KESC) peak gust was 37 mph before it stopped reporting.  Road closures included M-35 due to downed power lines.  Up to around 25% of customers without power.  Key Impacts: Very difficult to impossible travel, road closures, power outages.

The Munising ASOS (KP53) peak gust was 64 mph and the Newberry AWOS (KERY) reported a peak gust of 41 mph. Up to around 10% of customers without power.  Key Impacts: Very difficult to impossible travel, power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought a brief period of system snow followed by lake effect snow showers across southern Lower Michigan.  Total snow accumulations in the areas most impacted by lake effect snow showers ranged from 5 to 12 inches. The combination of snow and strong wind gusts produced widespread blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of system snow during the early morning of December 29th was followed by lake effect snow showers from mid morning through the evening hours. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 3 to 6 inches.  A report from emergency management of around 6 inches of snow was received near Three Rivers, MI.  The combination of the snow and strong wind gusts to 45 mph led to widespread blowing and drifting snow along with whiteout or near whiteout conditions at times. Numerous slide-offs and accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow occurred behind a cold front from the evening of the 18th to the early morning hours of the 19th, with totals ranging from 2 inches in the south-central UP to around 7 inches in the western UP. The snow, combined with temperatures plummeting below freezing, resulted in slippery roadways and school closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 7 inches of snow, combined with temperatures plummeting below freezing, resulted in slippery roadways from the evening of the 18th through the morning of the 19th. Key impacts: travel delays, school closures.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain resulted in up to 0.25 of ice accretion, causing slippery roads. Key impacts: transportation delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong low pressure system crossed northern Michigan during the overnight hours on the 28th into the 29th. Somewhat milder air ahead of the low led to some areas of rain/freezing rain during the overnight hours, before transitioning to snow as much colder air rushed into the region behind a cold front.  Highest snowfall totals ranged from 8-10 inches across NW lower Michigan to nearly 2 feet across western Chippewa and Mackinac counties. Sustained winds of 20-30mph were accompanied by widespread wind gusts of 40-50mph���including some stronger wind gusts to around 60-70mph early in the morning. Synoptic moisture departed through the afternoon and snowfall tapered off to NW lake effect bands through the afternoon as winds slowly diminished.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Upwards of 12-16 inches of snow had fallen across western Chippewa County by the morning of the 29th. Strong winds led to significant blowing and drifting of snow, to the extent road commissions struggled to keep roads clear. This resulted in the Chippewa County Road Commission issuing a statement on social media urging residents to stay home unless absolutely necessary. Final snowfall totals as high as 20-24inches. Wind gusts as high as 65mph were reported at Point Iroquois Light near Brimley, MI, prior to 05:00.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observers at NWS Gaylord, MI forecast office 9mi SSW of Gaylord, MI reported approximately 0.10in of radial ice between 19:00 and midnight on the 29th, with 9.1 inches of snow by 1pm, most of which fell between 07:00 and 13:00. 24hr snowfall total ending at midnight going into the 30th was 11.7 inches, which set a new record for the Gaylord area for this date. Widespread reports of 8-12 inches of total snowfall across the county by the morning of the 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low-pressure system brought windy conditions to Southeast Michigan, with peak wind gusts ranging from 45���55 mph. Isolated gusts reaching or exceeding 60 mph were also observed. Notable peak reports included: 60 mph in Port Hope (Huron County) at 5:14 PM; 59 mph in Pontiac (Oakland County) at 12:14 PM; 56 mph in Saginaw (Saginaw County) at 3:59 PM; 55 mph at Flint Bishop Airport (Genesee County) at 2:36 PM; and 54 mph at Detroit Metro Airport (Wayne County) at 7:20 AM. Fortunately, peak power outages remained under 20,000 customers; the lack of leaves on the trees helped limit overall tree damage. In addition to the high winds, localized snow squalls developed, which caused travel difficulities and disruptions.

Highest snowfall totals ranged from 8-10 inches across NW lower Michigan to nearly 2 feet across western Chippewa and Mackinac counties. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low-pressure system tracked across Lower Michigan from late December 9th through the morning of December 10th. Snowfall totals ranged from 1���3 inches south of M-59, while areas along and north of M-59 saw 3���6 inches, with isolated reports of 7 inches. Because temperatures hovered near freezing, the snow was heavy and wet with high liquid content; meanwhile, southern portions of the forecast area transitioned to rain as temperatures rose within the system's warm sector.||Some of the highest snowfall reports included 7.1 inches northwest of Saginaw (Saginaw County) and 7.0 inches in Sebewaing (Huron County). Precipitation tapered off in the afternoon as a cold front crossed Southeast Michigan, leading to a sharp temperature drop into the teens overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, and very moist air mass in the Midwest, combined with a front slowly leaning into the region, resulted in development of upstream convection that, even after decaying earlier in the day, was enough of an atmospheric perturbation to kick off another line of thunderstorms. This line crossed Lake Michigan during the late evening hours on the 23rd. This line resulted in gusty winds and thunderstorm wind damage, particularly across northwest Lower Michigan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Central dispatch reported significant power outages in the vicinity of Mesick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A highly unstable atmosphere over the Midwest, combined with a subtle boundary draping into the Upper Midwest on the morning of the 24th, resulted in development of a thunderstorm complex that crossed northern Lake Michigan and tracked through the Tip of the Mitt region. A mesovortex embedded within the complex tightened in the vicinity of Pellston, resulting in several reports of a possible funnel cloud in the vicinity of Weadock and Riggsville in rural, northern Cheboygan County though no tornado damage was discovered during the storm survey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees down with reports of a funnel cloud in the vicinity of Weadock in northern Cheboygan county. Additional reports and photos of a funnel and/or wall cloud all the way to the city of Cheboygan with this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread 6 to 12 inches of snow occurred on November 29-30. Isolated spots of over a foot occurred near the Lake Michigan shore.  Travel was significantly disrupted during the weekend after Thanksgiving.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 6 and 12 inches of snow fell countywide. Travel was significantly impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of snow north of a low pressure system moved into lower Michigan during the mid-late afternoon hours on the 29th. This continued through the night, with some east/southeast flow lake enhancement along the Lake Huron coastline slowly rotating back toward the coast toward morning as the surface low occluded across southern lower Michigan. An area of lake enhanced snowfall ultimately pivoted westward across the Tip of the Mitt during the morning hours, eventually congealing into an east-west oriented line of snow that swept southward across northern Lower Michigan through the middle of the day. Behind this, north flow lake effect snow took over. Highest snowfall totals were generally across northeast Lower Michigan, as well as the Cedarville area in the eastern U.P. where as much as 8 to 10 inches of snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of 10 inches near Clear Lake State Park in north central Montmorency county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Later in the afternoon on the 24th, a line of storms, associated with the morning storms across the Tip of the Mitt, developed across the southern/southeastern portions of the forecast area, resulting in pockets of tree damage from northeastern Gladwin county and Ogemaw county, eastward to Lake Huron.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of thunderstorm wind damage and power outages in neighborhoods near Grass Lake in northwestern Gladwin county. Trained spotter in this area reported personal weather station wind measurement to 51mph at 10 ft above ground level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy lake effect snow showers developed during the morning hours of November 9th and continued into the early morning hours of November 10th.  Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 14 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy lake effect snow fell from the afternoon on November 9th into the early morning hours of November 10th. Snowfall rates were briefly as high as 1 to 2 inches per hour with the heavier lake effect snow showers. Total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 11 inches. A report from a trained spotter of 10.5 inches of snow was received 1 mile south of Saint Joseph. The combination of snow and gusty north winds to 30 mph at times also created some blowing and drifting snow and hazardous travel conditions. Numerous slide-offs and accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure crossed the Upper Midwest Wednesday and Wednesday night. Rain changed to light snow through the day Wednesday as temperatures fell behind the initial cold front. Northwesterly winds strengthened dramatically behind the front, gusting into the 40-50mph range Wednesday night. Strong winds continued into Thanksgiving day, exacerbating impacts from intense northwest flow lake effect snow bands across northwest Lower Michigan in particular. The most prominent band stretched largely from the Norwood/Charlevoix area down through Mancelona/Lakes of the North into the Frederic area in Crawford county...occasionally stretching as far southeast as Arenac county due to the strong winds aloft. Reports of 14 to 15 inches began filtering in as early as 10-11am on Thanksgiving morning, with some reports of over 20 inches near Frederic by 1pm. This band continued to persist in the same general area through most of the day, only slowly shifting around into Thursday night. Additional lake effect snow fell during the day Friday, though intensity tapered off through the morning as the excessively favorable environment began to wane.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty-four hour snowfall total between midnight on the 27th and 28th at WFO Gaylord, 9mi SSW of the city of Gaylord, MI, was 13.1 inches, with a storm total of 19.6 inches. Areas just west and south of the office, particularly the Wilderness Valley and Waters area were more consistently beneath the more intense snow band, received nearly 20 inches of snow by the middle of the day on the 27th...with storm totals as high as 25inches. Snowfall totals varied dramatically across the county, with some areas (not under the band) receiving closer to 10 inches or less over the course of 72 hrs.

This line crossed Lake Michigan during the late evening hours on the 23rd. This line resulted in gusty winds and thunderstorm wind damage, particularly across northwest Lower Michigan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees reported down in the vicinity of Frankfort, MI, including South Shore Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first accumulating snowfall of the year occurred in multiple rounds from the evening of Saturday the 8th through the early afternoon of Monday the 10th for the north to northwest wind snow belts. Snowfall amounts in the most persistent bands ranged from 6 to 18 inches. Roads became snow covered and slippery, and the snow resulted in many schools and services being closed on Monday the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first accumulating snowfall of the year brought 2 to 5 inches to the area. Key impacts: transportation delays.

Additional lake effect snow fell during the day Friday, though intensity tapered off through the morning as the excessively favorable environment began to wane.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals by mid-morning on the 27th were as high as 20 inches. Highest storm total snowfall report near May Lake, southwest of the town of Frederic, was 23.3 inches. Reports continued to vary across the county depending on where the most intense band stayed persistent longest; areas closer to Lovells, only 15miles ENE of May Lake, reported a storm total of 11.5 inches. The cooperative observer in Grayling, MI measured a total of 17 inches by the morning of the 29th. NWS employee traveling south on Old 27 between Waters and Grayling during the morning of the 27th estimated at least 14 inches on the ground and whiteout conditions near Frederic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure crossed the Upper Midwest Wednesday and Wednesday night. Rain changed to light snow through the day Wednesday as temperatures fell behind the initial cold front. Northwesterly winds strengthened dramatically behind the front, gusting into the 40-50mph range Wednesday night. Strong winds continued into Thanksgiving day, exacerbating impacts from intense northwest flow lake effect snow bands across northwest Lower Michigan in particular. The most prominent band stretched largely from the Norwood/Charlevoix area down through Mancelona/Lakes of the North into the Frederic area in Crawford county...occasionally stretching as far southeast as Arenac county due to the strong winds aloft. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first accumulating snowfall of the year occurred in multiple rounds from the evening of Saturday the 8th through the early afternoon of Monday the 10th for the north to northwest wind snow belts. Snowfall amounts in the most persistent bands ranged from 6 to 18 inches. Roads became snow covered and slippery, and the snow resulted in many schools and services being closed on Monday the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first accumulating snowfall of the year brought 6 to 18 inches to the area, heaviest over the higher terrain. Key impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents, school closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system brought high wind and heavy snow to portions of the Upper Midwest and Great Lakes, significantly impacting travel during Thanksgiving week. Snowfall totals in the western and central UP ranged from 1 to 3 feet, and wind gusts of 40 to 60 mph caused blizzard conditions. The wind combined with initially wet snow stuck to trees resulted in downed trees and wires, causing around 14,000 customers in the western and central UP to lose power, some for as long as 30 hours. Several warming shelters opened for residents who lost power. Area snowmobile trails experienced severe damage due to downed trees, resulting in negative economic impact to area businesses. Non-essential travel was discouraged in portions of the western UP, and there were several reports of cars sliding off the roads. Lake effect snow continued into the Thanksgiving weekend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall totaling 1.5 to 2 feet along with winds gusting 40 to 60 miles per hour resulted in blizzard conditions. Twenty-seven percent of customers (around 1,300) lost power across the county. Key impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents, power outages, downed trees and wires, business closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep upper trough and attendant cold air settled into the eastern US to end the first full week of November, resulting in the first bout of accumulating lake effect snow of the 2025-26 winter season for northern Michigan. Light northerly winds initially led to the development of a subtle near-shore lake effect band over eastern Lake Michigan on the 8th, which just barely scraped parts of the Leelanau Peninsula at times. This congealed with a band over northern Lake Huron as a low pressure system tracked through the Ohio Valley, eventually drifting southward across the Tip of the Mitt and northwest Lower Michigan through the first part of the day on the 9th...ultimately diminishing with time by late in the evening...with a few north-flow lake effect bands across parts of Lake Michigan impacting areas generally near and south of Sleeping Bear Dunes, as well as areas downwind of Grand Traverse Bay. Highest snowfall totals ended up in the Leelanau Peninsula, where the lake effect band set up longest and had the best conditions for higher accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As much as 9.5 inches of snow fell over parts of west-central Grand Traverse county thanks to passage of a mesovortex during the early afternoon, followed by north-northeasterly flow lake effect in the late afternoon and evening hours on the 9th.

This congealed with a band over northern Lake Huron as a low pressure system tracked through the Ohio Valley, eventually drifting southward across the Tip of the Mitt and northwest Lower Michigan through the first part of the day on the 9th...ultimately diminishing with time by late in the evening...with a few north-flow lake effect bands across parts of Lake Michigan impacting areas generally near and south of Sleeping Bear Dunes, as well as areas downwind of Grand Traverse Bay. Highest snowfall totals ended up in the Leelanau Peninsula, where the lake effect band set up longest and had the best conditions for higher accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tip of the Leelanau Peninsula near Northport received as much as 9 inches of snow as early as mid-morning, thanks to off-shore band just barely nicking the tip of the peninsula. As the band moved inland, additional snow fell, with reports of as much as 10 inches at Stony Point, northeast of Suttons Bay on the east side of the Leelanau Peninsula.  Snow to liquid ratios were as high as 20 to 1.

Key impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents, power outages, downed trees and wires, business closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system brought high wind and heavy snow to portions of the Upper Midwest and Great Lakes, significantly impacting travel during Thanksgiving week. Snowfall totals in the western and central UP ranged from 1 to 3 feet, and wind gusts of 40 to 60 mph caused blizzard conditions. The wind combined with initially wet snow stuck to trees resulted in downed trees and wires, causing around 14,000 customers in the western and central UP to lose power, some for as long as 30 hours. Several warming shelters opened for residents who lost power. Area snowmobile trails experienced severe damage due to downed trees, resulting in negative economic impact to area businesses. Non-essential travel was discouraged in portions of the western UP, and there were several reports of cars sliding off the roads. Lake effect snow continued into the Thanksgiving weekend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow ranging from 4 to 12 inches (heaviest in the western part of the county) along with winds gusting 35 to 45 miles per hour, resulted in very difficult travel conditions due to accumulating and blowing snow. Key impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents, business closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 20 to 35 mph with gusts over 50 mph were common with a winter storm that brought 4 to 8 inches of snow to Lower Michigan November 26-27. This impacted holiday travel as the snow and wind started the day before Thanksgiving and continued through the Thanksgiving holiday. All forms of transportation were impacted by this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts over 45mph and snow for a good portion of 24 hours resulted in significant travel disruption and numerous accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of snow north of a low pressure system moved into lower Michigan during the mid-late afternoon hours on the 29th. This continued through the night, with some east/southeast flow lake enhancement along the Lake Huron coastline slowly rotating back toward the coast toward morning as the surface low occluded across southern lower Michigan. An area of lake enhanced snowfall ultimately pivoted westward across the Tip of the Mitt during the morning hours, eventually congealing into an east-west oriented line of snow that swept southward across northern Lower Michigan through the middle of the day. Behind this, north flow lake effect snow took over. Highest snowfall totals were generally across northeast Lower Michigan, as well as the Cedarville area in the eastern U.P. where as much as 8 to 10 inches of snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 8-12 inches near and north of Alpena where lake enhancement was maximized, downwind of Thunder Bay and Lake Huron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure brought 1 to 2 inches of snow across most of Southeast Michigan on the 9th. Cold air behind the system, paired with northeast winds, led to heavy lake-effect snow showers heading into the 10th. These showers produced localized accumulations of 3���7 inches across the Eastern Thumb region, particularly in Huron County, where the highest amounts occurred. Winds also gusted between 25 and 40 mph within the snow bands, leading to significant blowing and drifting. The highest totals recorded in Huron County included 7.5 inches in Ubly and 5.0 inches in Bad Axe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highest snowfall totals in Huron County were 7.5 inches in Ubly to 5.0 inches in Bad Axe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A post-Thanksgiving winter storm produced 3 to 8 inches of snow across most of Southeast Michigan. The highest totals, ranging from 5 to 8 inches, occurred over the Tri-Cities region. Notable reports included 7.8 inches in St. Charles (Saginaw County), 7.5 inches just west of Midland (Midland County), and 6.6 inches in Bay City (Bay County). The wet and heavy nature of the snow made roads very slippery. The Michigan State Police and local news outlets (like WDIV and CBS Detroit) noted that dozens of minor spin-outs and fender-benders occurred throughout the Metro Detroit area as the heaviest snow fell between 6:00 PM Saturday and 4:00 AM Sunday.||Milder air near Lake Erie and the Detroit and St. Clair rivers led to lower accumulations, with totals remaining under 3 inches for those immediate shoreline areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 7.5 inches just west of Midland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northward moving warm front interacted with limited instability and shear to produce a line of showers and thunderstorms. While most remained below severe levels, one storm gained sufficient strength to produce marginally severe hail.

A disturbance moved through the region during the afternoon and evening hours, producing showers and thunderstorms. A weakly sheared environment limited overall organization, but one storm did produce some large hail.

A low-pressure system tracked across Lower Michigan from late December 9th through the morning of December 10th. Snowfall totals ranged from 1���3 inches south of M-59, while areas along and north of M-59 saw 3���6 inches, with isolated reports of 7 inches. Because temperatures hovered near freezing, the snow was heavy and wet with high liquid content; meanwhile, southern portions of the forecast area transitioned to rain as temperatures rose within the system's warm sector.||Some of the highest snowfall reports included 7.1 inches northwest of Saginaw (Saginaw County) and 7.0 inches in Sebewaing (Huron County). Precipitation tapered off in the afternoon as a cold front crossed Southeast Michigan, leading to a sharp temperature drop into the teens overnight.
A few severe thunderstorms developed during the peak heating of the day, producing wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately 50 trees downed and some wires.
